vulnerability.title: Configure Multiple DNS Servers in /etc/resolv.conf
vulnerability.description: Determine whether the system is using local or DNS name resolution with the following command: $ sudo grep hosts /etc/nsswitch.conf hosts: files dns If the DNS entry is missing from the host's line in the "/etc/nsswitch.conf" file, the "/etc/resolv.conf" file must be empty. Verify the "/etc/resolv.conf" file is empty with the following command: $ sudo ls -al /etc/resolv.conf -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 0 Aug 19 08:31 resolv.conf If the DNS entry is found on the host's line of the "/etc/nsswitch.conf" file, then verify the following: Multiple Domain Name System (DNS) Servers should be configured in /etc/resolv.conf. This provides redundant name resolution services in the event that a domain server crashes. To configure the system to contain as least 2 DNS servers, add a corresponding nameserver ip_address entry in /etc/resolv.conf for each DNS server where ip_address is the IP address of a valid DNS server. For example: search example.com nameserver 192.168.0.1 nameserver 192.168.0.2
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Configure Multiple DNS Servers in /etc/resolv.conf

triage_decision.reason: Adding additional nameserver entries to /etc/resolv.conf is a straightforward text file edit that does not affect authentication, bootloader, or critical services. It is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage and can be validated quickly.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: networking affected; filesystems affected; network configuration; potential temporary DNS resolution disruption if entries are incorrect

reasoning_messages.reasoning: The rule requires at least two nameserver entries in /etc/resolv.conf. Check the current file for existing entries; if none, add multiple DNS servers. Verify nsswitch.conf has “hosts: files dns” so DNS resolution is enabled; if DNS is missing, resolv.conf must be empty. If DNS is present, ensure that resolv.conf contains multiple nameserver lines. This satisfies the finding.

review_feedback: The plan overwrites /etc/resolv.conf without backup, may discard existing directives (search, options), and does not account for resolv.conf being managed by NetworkManager or systemd-resolved. Using hard‑coded public DNS servers could be inappropriate for the environment and may cause DNS resolution failures, affecting services that depend on DNS (e.g., package management, authentication, SSH reverse lookup).

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan will address the requirement of having multiple DNS servers when DNS resolution is enabled and will clear resolv.conf when DNS is disabled, thus satisfying the vulnerability criteria.
concerns: The plan does not back up the original /etc/resolv.conf before overwriting it.
Using sed to replace the file in‑place may discard existing valid directives (e.g., search, options).
The plan does not account for environments where /etc/resolv.conf is managed by NetworkManager, systemd‑resolved, or is a symlink.
No validation is performed to ensure the added DNS IP addresses are reachable or appropriate for the host.
suggested_improvements: Create a backup of /etc/resolv.conf (e.g., cp /etc/resolv.conf /etc/resolv.conf.bak) before making changes.
Preserve existing non‑nameserver lines (search, options) when adding missing nameserver entries.
Detect if /etc/resolv.conf is a symlink or managed by a resolver service and use the appropriate tool (e.g., resolvconf, systemd-resolve) to modify DNS settings.
Validate that the chosen DNS server IPs are reachable (e.g., ping) or use organization‑approved DNS servers instead of public ones.
